data.title: Replica of alleged Trump Epstein birthday note appears on National Mall
data.description:
A large replica of a birthday note attributed to President Trump that was reportedly included in a book given to Jeffrey Epstein for his 50th birthday in 2003 appeared on the National Mall in Washington D.C. on Monday, a day before what would’ve been the convicted sex offender’s 73rd birthday.

A plaque inviting visitors to sign the card with a message to the Trump administration sits next to a box of Sharpies in front of the card. It asks people to refrain from “promotional, violent, or hateful speech” and notes such rhetoric would be removed.

The 10-foot-tall replica was erected by The Secret Handshake, according to multiple news outlets, an anonymous group responsible for several other statues depicting Trump and Epstein that have popped up at the same location in recent months, including one of the pair holding hands.  

“Kudos to these Trump Deranged Liberals for constantly inventing new ways to light Democrat donor money on fire by spreading fake news,” White House spokesperson Abigail Jackson said in a statement about the new installation. “When will they put up the statue about Democrats like Stacey Plaskett and Hakeem Jeffries who continued to solicit money and meetings from Epstein after he was a convicted sex offender.”

Trump has repeatedly denied writing the note, which was released by Democrats on the House Oversight Committee last September.

It shows the outline of a naked woman’s figure with a message in the middle that appears to be a scripted conversation between Trump and Epstein, referencing “certain things in common” between the two.

“A pal is a wonderful thing. Happy Birthday — may every day be another wonderful secret,” it reads.

Trump sued The Wall Street Journal, the first outlet to report about the alleged letter, earlier last summer. The White House has maintained that the president’s signature was a forgery.

Trump’s Department of Justice (DOJ) has faced growing pressure over its handling of files related to Epstein, with a new CNN poll finding that only 6 percent of Americans were satisfied with the amount of information that’s been released.

The poll was conducted about a month after the DOJ’s congressionally mandated December deadline to release all unclassified documents concerning Epstein and his longtime associate Ghislaine Maxwell, with redactions to protect the victims.

The same survey found that two-thirds of respondents said they thought the federal government was intentionally holding back some information that should be released.

The DOJ said in an early January court filing that it had released approximately 12,285 documents in accordance with the Epstein Transparency Act and was in the process of reviewing over 2 million more files.

data.title: Trump administration convening first critical minerals summit
data.description:
The Trump administration announced plans Tuesday to hold a critical minerals summit — a move that comes in the wake of President Trump’s ramped up pursuit of Greenland.

“On February 4, @SecRubio will welcome partners from across the globe to the State Department for the inaugural Critical Minerals Ministerial,” the department posted on social platform X.

“Strengthening critical mineral supply chains with our international partners is vital to America’s economic and national security, technological leadership, and a resilient energy future,” the post reads.

Trump and his administration have maintained that the takeover of Greenland is critical to U.S. national security and to stave off threats from China and Russia in the Arctic. The island, a semi-autonomous territory of Denmark, also has vast mineral resources.

Stocks on Tuesday fell as Trump leans on Denmark and its NATO allies to forge a deal on the acquisition. The Dow had fallen by close to 700 points by mid-morning, the S&P 500 had dipped by over 1.4 percent and the Nasdaq dropped by 1.7 percent.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent on Tuesday warned Europe against imposing retaliatory tariffs after the president announced additional import taxes on Denmark and eight other nations over their pushback on Trump’s plan to acquire Greenland.

Bessent, in a conversation with Fox Business’s Maria Bartiromo at the World Economic Forum in Switzerland, told European leaders should “sit back [and] take a deep breath.”

“Do not retaliate,” he added.

California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) on Tuesday advised the Europeans to “buck up” amid the Trump administration’s pressure.

“Do you have a message for Europeans who are concerned about the messages from the White House around Greenland this week?” Sky News’s Paul Kelso asked Newsom.

“Yeah, it’s time to buck up,” Newsom replied. “It’s time to get serious. And stop being complicit. It’s time to stand tall and firm, have a backbone. You know, I’ve seen this in the United States, supine Congress, playing both sides. You know, say one thing on a text or a tweet, another publicly.”

data.title: Nationwide walkouts planned as Trump marks 1 year back in office
data.description:
(NewsNation) — Tens of thousands of people were expected to take part in protests nationwide Tuesday as opposition to President Donald Trump’s first year back in office builds into a coordinated movement.

Free America Walkout organizers urged people to walk out of work, school and to avoid shopping as a form of protest against the administration’s policies during the first year of Trump’s second term.

The demonstrations are aimed at Immigration and Customs Enforcement raids, presidential policies affecting immigrants and transgender people, and increased government surveillance. The goal, organizers said, is to create disruption that forces the administration to take notice.

More than 800 walkouts planned nationwide

Organizers said more than 800 events were planned across all 50 states, with over 38,000 people signed up for rallies stretching from Tampa to Los Angeles to New York City.

At 2 p.m. local time Tuesday, protesters nationwide plan to step away from their daily routines as part of what’s called the “Free America” movement. The group’s slogan: “In the face of fascism, we will be ungovernable.”

Organizers said the action is designed to interrupt normal routines, not just draw crowds. 

The demonstrations come in the wake of the fatal shooting of Renee Nicole Macklin Good, 37, by an ICE officer in Minnesota, which organizers argue has become a rallying point.

The Women’s March, one of the groups behind the effort, said walkouts are also planned internationally, including in Canada, France, Italy and the Netherlands.

With tensions still high around immigration protests, questions remain about public safety. Officials said they were expecting peaceful protests, and the Women’s March said it has a clear nonviolence policy in place.

Protests have surpassed those in Trump’s first term, data shows

Last summer’s “No Kings” demonstrations drew an estimated 5 million people nationwide. While most demonstrations were peaceful, some clashes led to arrests, serious injuries and one death. Officials have stressed that those incidents were the exception, not the rule.

In the year since Trump’s second term began, the number of protests in the U.S. has surpassed those seen at the same point in his first administration, according to data from the Crowd Counting Consortium.

According to the data, more than 10,700 protests occurred in 2025, a 133% increase from the 4,588 recorded during Trump’s first term in 2017. A majority of U.S. counties, including 42% that voted for Trump, have seen at least one protest since his second inauguration, the data showed.

Organizers said Tuesday’s actions will serve as a test of whether weekday walkouts can apply meaningful pressure on the Trump administration.

Tuesday marks one year since Trump was sworn in for his second term. NewsNation will air a special edition of “CUOMO” with analysis from Stephen A. Smith and Bill O’Reilly at 8 p.m. EST.

data.title: Macron wears aviators during Davos speech pushing back on Trump
data.description:
French President Emmanuel Macron donned aviator sunglasses during his address to global leaders at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland on Tuesday. 

In his speech, the French head of state criticized the Trump administration’s tariffs and economic policies, accusing President Trump of actions that “openly aim to weaken and subordinate Europe.”

During a New Year’s oration to the country’s military last week, Macron addressed his “unsightly” red and puffy eye, assuring the crowd the condition was “completely harmless.”

“Simply see an unintentional reference to the ‘Eye of the Tiger’ … For those who catch the reference, it is a sign of determination,” Macron said, in apparent reference to the American band Survivor’s hit song. 

Macron’s Tuesday speech reflected rising tensions in Europe over tariffs imposed by the Trump administration on the countries’ goods. 

The Trump administration announced its plans last week to place a 10 percent import tax on all goods from France, Denmark, Sweden, Norway, Germany, the United Kingdom and the Netherlands. 

The tariffs, which are slated to go into effect on Feb. 1, followed opposition from these countries to Trump’s announced intentions to acquire the Danish territory of Greenland. 

Additionally, Trump has threatened to impose an additional 200 percent tariff on French wine and champagne after reports that Macron was not interested in joining Trump’s “Board of Peace.” 

Early Tuesday morning, Trump posted screenshots of messages between himself and Macron on Truth Social. In these private messages, Macron questioned the president’s threats to take Greenland and offered to host a G7 summit meeting in Paris on Thursday.

Trump is expected to sign the charter for the “Board of Peace” on Thursday morning in Davos.

data.title: Watch live: White House holds press briefing as Trump ramps up Greenland rhetoric
data.description:
White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt will brief reporters on the first year of President Trump’s second term as the administration ramps up its push to acquire Greenland from Denmark.

“In just one year, President Trump has accomplished more than many presidents do in eight. We’ve never had a president fight harder to deliver on the promises he made to the American people than President Trump,” Leavitt wrote Tuesday on social platform X. “I’ll recap it all during the Press Briefing today at 1PM. Tune in!”

Trump is expected to travel to Davos, Switzerland, later Tuesday to attend the World Economic Forum. He announced overnight that he agreed to meet with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte and European allies who have pushed back against the U.S. taking over Greenland.

On Saturday, the president said a 10 percent tariff would be imposed on exports from Denmark and seven of its allies — including France and the U.K. — starting Feb. 1 and could later be raised to 25 percent. He added that the import taxes would remain until a deal to acquire the Arctic island is reached.

The press briefing is scheduled to begin at 1 p.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: For Trump, life's a game of monopoly
data.description:
For young people, there’s always been a ready mode of escape from reality. Before Facebook, there were video games. But back in the days when Donald Trump was growing up, there were board games: Clue, Checkers, Scrabble, and, especially, that most popular of all board games — Monopoly.

Of course, most of us grew out of those board games. But not Donald Trump. His entire professional life has been a high-stakes game of Monopoly: rolling the dice, buying and trading properties, with borrowed money, starting at the low end and building up to the most expensive properties available — not because they always made sense, but simply because he wanted them.

Sometimes those gambles worked out, like Trump Tower and Mar-a-Lago. Sometimes they didn’t, like the Plaza Hotel and Atlantic City casinos. But Trump continued to play the game. And now he’s rolling the dice for the biggest Monopoly deal of all: Greenland. 

For Trump, making Greenland his latest property acquired is not just another real estate deal, it’s an absolute obsession. The United States must have “full control” of Greenland, he trumpeted on Truth Social. “Anything less is unacceptable.” 

He talks about it every day. He insists he’ll buy it, even though both Denmark and Greenland contend it’s not for sale. If he can’t buy it, he says he’s willing to take it by military force, even though doing so would violate the NATO Charter and become the first time in 80 years that one NATO member has invaded the territory of another.  

OK, I admit that I have no evidence to prove that Little Donnie ever played Monopoly, or that President Trump’s passion for adding Greenland to his long list of acquired properties is nothing but a continuation of his boyhood fixation with Monopoly. But my theory makes more sense than any reason he gives for seizing Greenland.

National security. We need more troops in Greenland, Trump says, to keep us safe – an assertion for which he provides no proof. But, even if true, under a 1951 treaty with Denmark, the United States can send as many troops to Greenland as we want. During the Cold War, we had 13 army bases and four naval bases in Greenland. After the collapse of the Soviet Union, we shut down all but one, Pituffik Space Base, which still exists today. We could open more bases or send more troops to Greenland anytime we want.  

Russia and China. Trump argues we need to take over Greenland to stop Russia or China from doing so. This is all Trump’s imagination. Greenland officials insist they’ve had zero contact with China and, since the Cold War, no involvement with Russia, either. The only threat Greenland faces today is not from China or Russia. It’s from the United States.  

Golden Dome. The president argues we need Greenland to construct the famous “Golden Dome” national missile-defense system he’s been talking about, but doing nothing about, for years. It makes no sense. Why do we need Greenland to protect the United States from incoming missiles? And if we need Greenland, wouldn’t we need Canada, too? President Reagan never claimed Greenland was essential for the “Strategic Defense Initiative,” his earlier version of “Golden Dome.” Reagan didn’t need Greenland. Neither does Trump.  

Mineral Deposits. Trump says we need to tap Greenland’s vast mineral wealth, which is easier said than done because they lie north of the Arctic Circle below mile-thick polar ice. But, again, even if American firms want to mine those minerals, Greenland’s not opposed. “I don’t see a need for taking over Greenland. We’re open to investment and working with Americans,” Christian Keldsen, managing director of the Greenland Business Association, told CNN.  

Indeed, outside his loyal toadies in the White House, not many agree with Trump’s Greenland fixation. According to the latest CBS poll, 86 percent of Americans oppose the use of military force to take Greenland. So does every other member nation of NATO. And, perhaps surprisingly, so do a growing number of Republican members of Congress. 

“It’s the dumbest thing I’ve ever heard of,” said Rep. Don Bacon (R-Neb), predicting it would lead to impeachment. If there ever were an illegal taking of Greenland, Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) warned, “there’d be sufficient numbers here to pass a war powers resolution and withstand a veto.” 

The fact is, economically or militarily, there is nothing the United States could get by taking over Greenland we don’t already have unlimited access to. The entire plan is a fool’s errand. 

Bill Press is host of “The Bill Press Pod.” He is the author of “From the Left: A Life in the Crossfire.”

data.title: Macron: Trump wants to 'weaken and subordinate' Europe
data.description:
French President Emmanuel Macron on Tuesday accused President Trump of wanting to “weaken and subordinate” Europe. 

Macron made the comments in front of global leaders at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. The remarks came amid a furor over Trump’s push to seize Greenland and his vow to place tariffs on imports from Denmark and seven other European Union allies who have rallied around Denmark, a NATO ally that controls Greenland.

“It’s clear that we are reaching a time of instability, of imbalances, both from the security and defense point of view and from the economic point of view,” Macron said.

Macron called the present moment “a very concerning time” in international cooperation. 

“Competition from the United States of America through trade agreements that undermine our export interests, demand maximum concessions and openly aim to weaken and subordinate Europe, combined with an endless accumulation of new tariffs that are fundamentally unacceptable, even more so when they are used so as leverage against territorial sovereignty,” Macron said. 

The remarks also came after Trump posted screenshots on Truth Social early Tuesday morning of messages Macron and other foreign leaders had sent him.

The Macron text chain shows the French president telling Trump, “I do not understand what you are doing on Greenland” and offering to set up a G7 meeting in Paris on Thursday. The U.S. president is set to sign the charter for his “Board of Peace” on Thursday morning. 

Additionally, after Macron said he was not ready yet to accept an invitation to join Trump’s board, the American president threatened to impose a “200% tariff” on French Champagne and wine. 

France has already been included in the Trump administration’s retaliatory tariffs against European countries who opposed his bid to acquire Greenland. The U.S. plans to impose a 10% import tax on all goods from France, Denmark, Sweden, Norway, Germany, the United Kingdom and the Netherlands. 

In his speech in Davos on Tuesday, Macron also appeared to mock Trump’s claims that he has solved eight wars during his time in office. 

“Look at the situation, where we are, I mean, a shift towards autocracy against democracy, more violence, more than 60 wars in 2024, an absolute record, even if I understood a few of them were fixed, and — and conflict has become normalized, hybrid, expanding into new demands — space, digital, information, cyber, trade and so on,” Macron said.

data.title: EU chief: Proposed Trump tariffs over Greenland a 'mistake'
data.description:
Ursula von der Leyen, the president of the European Commission, criticized President Trump for proposing tariffs on European Union (EU) nations over their support of Greenland’s sovereignty.

“Artic security can only be achieved together, and this is why the proposed additional tariffs are a mistake, especially between long-standing allies,” von der Leyen said Tuesday at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. “The European Union and the United States have agreed to a trade deal last July. 

“And in politics, as in business, a deal is a deal. And when friends shake hands, it must mean something.”

Trump, who will head to Switzerland for the summit Tuesday evening, announced Saturday that imports from Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, United Kingdom, the Netherlands and Finland will be subject to 10 percent tariffs starting Feb. 1. The levies will increase to 25 percent on June 1.

The president added that the import taxes will be in place until Denmark and Greenland agree to a deal that grants the U.S. control of the mineral-rich island. 

“We have subsidized Denmark, and all of the Countries of the European Union, and others, for many years by not charging them tariffs, or any other forms of remuneration,” Trump said. “Now, after centuries, it is time for Denmark to give back — World Peace is at stake!”

Von der Leyen and Trump previously reached a trade deal between the EU and U.S. last July. Under the agreement, European imports into the U.S. faced 15 percent tariffs, while the EU agreed to purchase $750 billion worth of energy from the U.S. and invest an additional $600 billion in the U.S. for other goods. 

“I think it’s the biggest deal ever made,” the president claimed at the time. Von der Leyen, meanwhile, said the deal would “bring stability, it will bring predictability.”

European leaders, including von der Leyen, pushed back Saturday against Trump’s proposed levies. The European Commission president wrote on the social platform X the tariffs “would undermine transatlantic relations and risk a dangerous downward spiral.” 

“Europe will remain united, coordinated, and committed to upholding its sovereignty,” she added.

Days before the president unveiled the new import taxes, European nations sent troops to Greenland as part of a joint exercise led by the Danish military. The buildup comes after Denmark boosted its military presence on the island last summer in an effort to strengthen Europe’s “footprint” in the Arctic.

data.title: What's behind Trump's delay on attacking Iran?
data.description:
Just over two weeks ago, in a “one-day war,” the U.S. launched a mission that seized Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro and his wife. As of a few days ago, in response to President Trump’s threat to acquire Greenland, several NATO allies deployed a small number of forces to prevent its NATO partner from invading a fellow NATO partner.  

Given the bizarre nature of these events, it’s hard to believe they actually happened.

What hasn’t happened is another question. Why has the U.S. not yet attacked Iran to protect the Iranians rioting in protest against an incompetent and autocratic government? Let’s pose an entirely speculative thought experiment to address that question.  

First, press reports have been vague as to why no strike has occurred. Trump was adamant. Many, myself included, believed that when the U.S. had sufficient forces in the region, an attack would be launched. For the time being, Trump has backed off that threat arguing that the mullahs in Tehran are no longer killing their citizens protesting their rule. 

Whether that assessment is correct or not, it makes sense that a president would declare victory if they believed the killing had stopped. But let us speculate a bit more.

No doubt Trump was serious in making that threat. With the success of Maduro’s capture behind him and the assurance of America’s military prowess in the Midnight Hammer missions last June that hammered Iran’s nuclear infrastructure, taking on the ayatollahs would seem easy. But what might his advisors cautioned?

Before Midnight Hammer and in his first term, Trump halted a retaliatory hit against Iran after it had shot down a U.S. Reaper spy drone. Why? The reason given was the possibility of collateral civilian casualties. Would that be repeated if the U.S. struck a range of Iranian targets?

Someone might have briefed the president on Operation Eagle Claw, the failed 1980 hostage rescue mission to free U.S. citizens held in the former U.S. embassy in Tehran. That fiasco ended in disaster almost before it started, with a collision between one of the mission’s CH-46 helicopters and a C-130 refueled at Desert One in a remote part of Iran. 

Operation Eagle Claw, in part, cost Jimmy Carter the 1980 election. With U.S. elections in November, would Trump risk another failure? While there were not sufficient forces in the Gulf to assure a successful strike to halt the violence, no doubt planning was completed on what to hit and what not.  

One can assume that cyberattacks to immobilize Iranian defenses, much as in Venezuela, would be carried out. Attempts to restore the internet or not would be considered. And no doubt greater sanctions against the mullahs and the Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps would be imposed. But what other targets might be struck?

The fundamental question must be: What is the strategic purpose of any action in Iran, kinetic or otherwise? Is it to prevent the further slaughter of Iranians? Is it to foment a regime change? Is it to punish the theocracy? Or was it to force the mullahs from further violence against the public?

Given the broad nature of the riots, preventing further violence would require striking many targets, presumably of the Revolutionary Guard Corps and its Basij units. Although the Quds force primarily acts abroad to support allies, it might be called into action against civilians. So it is unclear how, other than punitive strikes, a sufficient number of regime elements could be targeted to stop violence against protesting Iranians.

On regime change, who would govern once the mullahs were out? There is no sign of an internal government that is ready to take charge. If external leaders such as Crown Prince Reza Pahlavi, the shah’s son, were to return — as was tried in Iraq — the chances of success would be slim. And the Revolutionary Guard Corps might be even more radical than the clerics in putting down any protests and even restarting the nuclear program. And punishing the regime made little sense.

So what is the administration’s Iran policy today? While awaiting the arrival of a carrier strike group to the Gulf, here is a radical idea. Why not negotiate with the mullahs to determine if the regime will be prepared to allow more freedoms to its people? Can the nuclear agreement be restarted? And can Iran return to the international community? 

Perhaps the answers are no. But why not try?

Harlan Ullman, Ph.D., is UPI’s Arnaud deBorchgrave Distinguished Columnist, a senior advisor at the Atlantic Council, the chairman of two private companies and the principal author of the doctrine of shock and awe. He and former United Kingdom Defense Chief David Richards are the authors of a forthcoming book on preventing strategic catastrophe.

data.title: House panel opens Minnesota fraud investigation
data.description:
The House Energy and Commerce Committee announced Tuesday it has opened a social services fraud investigation into the state of Minnesota.

“The extensive fraud schemes being perpetrated in Minnesota have wreaked havoc on government-funded health programs. We have an obligation to ensure finite taxpayer dollars are being used responsibly, and that the most vulnerable Americans are not being exploited to the benefit of fraudsters and foreign actors,” Committee Chair Brett Guthrie (R-Ky.) and GOP Reps. John Joyce (Pa.) and Morgan Griffith (Va.) said in a statement announcing the investigation.

“As members of Congress and this Committee, our track record has made our continued commitment to ridding government programs of waste, fraud, mismanagement, and abuse clear. This letter is the next step in the Committee’s work to root out fraud and restore program integrity in our federal health programs nationwide,” they added. 

A source told The Hill’s sister channel NewsNation that the probe will focus on state-administered Medicaid programs in an “industrial scale” investigation.

Close

Thank you for signing up!

Subscribe to more newsletters here

The latest in politics and policy. Direct to your inbox. Sign up for the Morning Report newsletter

The GOP-led panel plans to push Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) to provide by the end of the month details on all audits that relate to 14 social services programs that the state previously deemed to be prone to “high-risk provider types,” Fox News Digital first reported.

Lawmakers are demanding documents, audits and records dating back to 2019 seeking answers on whether state officials should have been able to recognize, catch or prevent fraudulent schemes.

“Ensuring Medicaid program integrity is critical to preserving access to vital health care services for those that need it most,” the GOP lawmakers said in a letter to Walz.

“Every dollar stolen from the Medicaid program by fraudsters is taken from children, pregnant women, the elderly, and people with disabilities,” it added.

The Minnesota governor will also be required to produce all communications with state Medicaid Commissioner John Connolly.

“Many of Minnesota’s Medicaid programs were designed in such a way that made them
vulnerable to fraud. The Housing Stabilization Support (HSS) program was established to “help people with disabilities and seniors find and keep housing in their own home,'” the letter states.

“Instead of directly providing housing to those in need, HSS paid consultants to help people find housing, attracting fraudsters from all over the U. S. to register as HSS providers with low barriers to entry and little oversight over reimbursements,” it added.

Guthrie alleged Walz’s administration “willfully ignored” whistleblower reports, further preventing the fraud from being uncovered.

“If Minnesota fails to comply with federal Medicaid requirements, CMS [Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services] will begin withholding Medicaid funds from the state until it comes into compliance and implements a corrective action plan to address rampant FWA [fraud, waste and abuse],” the letter states.

The Hill has reached out Walz’s office for comment.

The committee investigation follows the departure of Joseph H. Thompson, who resigned from his position as the First Assistant U.S. Attorney in the U.S. Attorney’s Office for the District of Minnesota after being tasked with overseeing the federal investigation into allegations that social services programs in the state defrauded the federal government of millions of dollars.

Walz, who has dropped his reelection bid amid the fraud controversy in Minnesota, called the prosecutor’s departure “a huge loss for our state” and alleged it was the latest instance of the administration “pushing nonpartisan career professionals out of the Justice Department, replacing them with his sycophants.”
